---
title: "Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets"
subtitle: "R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Reshape data between **wide** and **long** formats with `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()`
- Combine datasets using **joins**: `left_join()`, `inner_join()`, `full_join()`
- Stack datasets with `bind_rows()`
- Perform basic **string operations** with `stringr` (cleaning facility names, extracting patterns)
:::

## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">Wide vs<br>Long</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">pivot_<br>longer()</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">pivot_<br>wider()</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Joins</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Strings</div>
</div>


# Wide vs. Long Format {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The shape of your data matters</span>


## Two Ways to Store the Same Data

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**Wide format** (good for reading)

| facility_id | jan_deliveries | feb_deliveries | mar_deliveries |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| F001 | 45 | 52 | 48 |
| F002 | 120 | 115 | 130 |
:::

::: {.col}
**Long format** (good for analysis and ggplot2)

| facility_id | month | deliveries |
|-------------|-------|------------|
| F001 | jan | 45 |
| F001 | feb | 52 |
| F001 | mar | 48 |
| F002 | jan | 120 |
| F002 | feb | 115 |
| F002 | mar | 130 |
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Most R analysis and visualization functions (dplyr, ggplot2) expect **long format**. Most spreadsheet exports from DHIS2 and Excel arrive in **wide format**. Learning to reshape between them is an essential skill.
:::


# pivot_longer() {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Wide to long</span>


## pivot_longer() --- Unpivoting

```{r}
#| label: pivot-longer-basic
#| echo: true

library(tidyr)

# Wide format: monthly delivery data
wide_data <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003"),
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"),
  jan = c(45, 120, 33),
  feb = c(52, 115, 38),
  mar = c(48, 130, 35)
)

# Pivot to long format
long_data <- wide_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = jan:mar,           # columns to pivot
    names_to = "month",        # new column for the old column names
    values_to = "deliveries"   # new column for the values
  )

long_data
```


## Practical MNH Example

```{r}
#| label: pivot-longer-mnh
#| echo: true

# Quarterly indicators from DHIS2 (wide format export)
quarterly <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003"),
  nmr_q1 = c(22.1, 31.7, 45.3),
  nmr_q2 = c(20.8, 29.5, 42.0),
  nmr_q3 = c(19.5, 28.1, 38.8),
  nmr_q4 = c(18.2, 27.0, 36.5)
)

# Reshape for trend analysis
quarterly_long <- quarterly |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("nmr_"),
    names_to = "quarter",
    names_prefix = "nmr_",     # strip the "nmr_" prefix
    values_to = "nmr"
  )

quarterly_long
# Now ready for ggplot2 trend lines!
```


# pivot_wider() {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Long to wide</span>


## pivot_wider() --- Spreading

```{r}
#| label: pivot-wider
#| echo: true

# Long format: indicator values per country
indicators_long <- tibble(
  country = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 3),
  indicator = rep(c("nmr", "mmr", "anc_coverage"), 3),
  value = c(25.3, 353, 0.62,
            31.7, 524, 0.51,
            19.8, 439, 0.71)
)

# Spread to wide format for a summary table
indicators_wide <- indicators_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = indicator,
    values_from = value
  )

indicators_wide
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
Use `pivot_wider()` when you need a summary table for a report or presentation --- one row per country, one column per indicator.
:::


# Joining Datasets {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Combining data from multiple sources</span>


## Why Join?

In MNH program analysis, data lives in separate files:

::: {.incremental}
- Facility characteristics (one file)
- Monthly delivery data (another file)
- Country-level indicators (yet another file)
- Training records (yet another)
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Joins let you combine these into one analysis-ready dataset.** Instead of manually copying columns between spreadsheets, you write one line of code that matches rows by a shared key.
:::


## left_join() --- The Most Common Join

```{r}
#| label: left-join
#| echo: true

# Facility data
facilities <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004"),
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya"),
  facility_type = c("Hospital", "Health Center", "Health Post", "Hospital")
)

# Country-level indicators
country_data <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique"),
  gdp_per_capita = c(936, 1136, 625, 504),
  health_expenditure_pct = c(3.3, 3.6, 9.3, 4.9)
)

# Join: add country data to each facility
facilities |>
  left_join(country_data, by = "country")
# Kenya gets NA (no match in country_data)
# Mozambique is dropped (no match in facilities)
```


## Types of Joins

| Join | Keeps | Use Case |
|------|-------|----------|
| `left_join(x, y)` | All rows from x, matching from y | Add context to your main dataset |
| `inner_join(x, y)` | Only rows that match in both | Analysis requiring complete data |
| `full_join(x, y)` | All rows from both | Comprehensive audit of both sources |
| `anti_join(x, y)` | Rows in x with NO match in y | Find missing or unmatched records |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Start with `left_join()`.** It preserves every row in your primary dataset and adds columns from the second. You won't accidentally drop records.
:::


## Joining Monthly Data

```{r}
#| label: join-monthly
#| echo: true

# Monthly deliveries
monthly <- tibble(
  facility_id = rep(c("F001", "F002"), each = 3),
  month = rep(c("Jan", "Feb", "Mar"), 2),
  deliveries = c(45, 52, 48, 120, 115, 130)
)

# Add facility characteristics to monthly data
monthly_enriched <- monthly |>
  left_join(facilities, by = "facility_id")

monthly_enriched
# Now each monthly row has country and facility_type attached
```


## bind_rows() --- Stacking Datasets

```{r}
#| label: bind-rows
#| echo: true

# Separate quarterly data files (common DHIS2 pattern)
q1_data <- tibble(facility_id = c("F001", "F002"),
                   quarter = "Q1", deliveries = c(135, 365))
q2_data <- tibble(facility_id = c("F001", "F002"),
                   quarter = "Q2", deliveries = c(148, 350))
q3_data <- tibble(facility_id = c("F001", "F002"),
                   quarter = "Q3", deliveries = c(141, 378))

# Stack them into one dataset
annual_data <- bind_rows(q1_data, q2_data, q3_data)
annual_data
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
`bind_rows()` is your tool when you have the **same columns** split across multiple files --- quarterly DHIS2 exports, monthly facility reports, or country-specific datasets. It stacks them vertically into one dataset.
:::


## anti_join() --- Finding Gaps

```{r}
#| label: anti-join
#| echo: true

# Which facilities didn't report this month?
all_facilities <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004", "F005")
)

reported <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F003", "F005"),
  deliveries = c(45, 33, 120)
)

# Facilities that did NOT report
all_facilities |>
  anti_join(reported, by = "facility_id")
# F002 and F004 — follow up with these facilities
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
`anti_join()` is invaluable for **data quality audits** --- finding facilities that missed a reporting period, countries missing from a reference table, or patients lost to follow-up.
:::


# String Basics with stringr {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Cleaning messy text data</span>


## Common String Problems

```{r}
#| label: string-basics
#| echo: true

library(stringr)

# Facility names from DHIS2 are often inconsistent
facility_names <- c("Addis Ababa Hospital", "addis ababa hospital",
                     "  Dar es Salaam HC  ", "Lilongwe Health Centre")

# Convert to consistent case
str_to_title(facility_names)

# Remove leading/trailing whitespace
str_trim(facility_names)

# Detect a pattern
str_detect(facility_names, "Hospital|hospital")

# Replace text
str_replace(facility_names, "HC", "Health Center")
```


## Cleaning Facility Names

```{r}
#| label: string-cleaning
#| echo: true

facility_data |>
  mutate(
    # Standardize facility names
    facility_name = facility_name |>
      str_trim() |>
      str_to_title() |>
      str_replace_all("Hc\\b", "Health Center") |>
      str_replace_all("Hp\\b", "Health Post")
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Clean string data early.** Inconsistent names (like "HC" vs "Health Center") will cause joins to fail and `group_by()` to split what should be one group into many.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**`pivot_longer()` reshapes wide DHIS2 exports into the long format that R's analysis tools expect.**

**`left_join()` is your primary tool for combining facility data with country indicators, training records, and other reference tables.**

**Clean strings early --- inconsistent names break joins and grouping.**
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 6 --- Data Visualization with ggplot2**

- The grammar of graphics: data, aesthetics, and geometries
- Bar charts, scatter plots, line charts, and histograms
- Mapping variables to color, size, and shape
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[R for Management Consultants | Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
